title: They Saved Lisas Brain

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer driving and something goes wrong (tires screeching)
jokes.punchline: Classic Homer crash with his signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Iconic Homer moment but completely predictable setup. High character integration and quotability but low originality.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Shut up-a your mouth!
jokes.setup: Generic ethnic comedy stereotype on TV
jokes.punchline: Exaggerated Italian accent delivery
jokes.explanation: Lazy ethnic stereotype humor with no cleverness or subversion

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: And now, here's Bongo the Italian TV character!

dialogue.speaker: Italian TV character
dialogue.line: Mamma mia! Why-a you no call-a your mother? She's-a worried sick about you! Ay-oh!

jokes.text: please enjoy this encore presentation of Princess Di's funeral
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate rebroadcast of tragic event as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about media exploitation, well-timed but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program for an emergency broadcast.

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: Due to the tragic events, we will now rebroadcast last week's entertainment special.

jokes.text: I've never heard so many Viagra jokes in 30 seconds
jokes.setup: Homer enjoying the cancelled ethnic comedy
jokes.punchline: Meta observation about lazy comedy writing density
jokes.explanation: Homer's appreciation for lowbrow comedy fits his character while critiquing hack writing

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hoo-hoo! Look at all these lazy stereotypes! This is the kind of comedy writing I can really sink my teeth into.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why, they've packed more offensive clichés into one episode than the actual Simpsons uses in a whole season!

jokes.text: the low fat pudding that's approved for sale by the government
jokes.punchline: Damning with faint praise - 'approved for sale' is bare minimum
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of food marketing language - 'approved for sale' is hilariously minimal endorsement

dialogue.speaker: Radio Announcer
dialogue.line: New Nutri-Matic health drink! It's approved for sale in all fifty states!

jokes.text: Grandma Plopwell, you've done it again!
jokes.punchline: Addressing the pudding company as if it's a beloved entertainer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - treating corporate sponsor as personal friend

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, a pudding company sponsoring a gross-out contest! This is the greatest day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thank you, pudding company. You've made all my dreams come true. I love you.

jokes.text: Our new hyper sugar gives you 300% of your minimum daily sweetness requirement
jokes.punchline: Oxymoronic 'hyper sugar' in 'low fat' product plus made-up nutritional metric
jokes.explanation: Sharp food industry satire with absurd nutritional claim structure

dialogue.speaker: Grandma Plopwell representative
dialogue.line: Our new low-fat cookie is made with hyper sugar, a revolutionary ingredient that lets you eat twice as much guilt-free!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Hyper sugar? What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Grandma Plopwell representative
dialogue.line: It's sugar that's so active it burns calories just sitting in your stomach. Plus it's full of Mega-Nutrients, which are like regular nutrients, but mega.

jokes.text: Well, you do now
jokes.setup: Do you suffer from diabetes? No.
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that their product just gave him diabetes
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy with perfect timing and economy - instant diabetes as casual side effect

dialogue.speaker: Grandma Plopwell representative
dialogue.line: Do you suffer from diabetes?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Grandma Plopwell representative
dialogue.line: You will.

jokes.text: Our most disgusting contestant will win a free trip to Hartford, Connecticut
jokes.punchline: Complete downgrade from promised Hawaii to least appealing destination
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection payoff with Hartford as comedically disappointing destination

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: Congratulations! You've won our grand prize trip!

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: You're going to... Gary, Indiana!

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: But you said Hawaii!

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: I said 'Hi, uh, way ago.' Gary, Indiana!

jokes.text: I thought it was Hawaii. - No one said Hawaii.
jokes.punchline: DJ's blatant lie contradicting what we just heard
jokes.explanation: Solid gaslighting humor showing casual dishonesty in media contests

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: You're our lucky caller! You've won an all-expense paid trip to...

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: ...the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I misspoke. That was the Springfield Tire Fire.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Ladles and jelly spoons
jokes.punchline: Kitchen utensil puns on 'ladies and gentlemen'
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay fitting Bart's showman personality but fairly obvious

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the show!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Or as I like to call you... Ladles and Gentle-men!

jokes.text: Please, no more spark plugs
jokes.setup: Bart swallowing anything thrown at him
jokes.punchline: Reveals crowd is throwing dangerous car parts
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of absurdity showing how far the degradation has gone

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Bring it on! I can swallow anything!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? How about this?

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Yeah! Swallow it, Bart!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No problem!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Alright, here comes a bumper!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: A transmission! A engine block! A whole chassis!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ugh... I don't feel so good...

jokes.text: Unbelievable. We're rewarding people for acting like buffoons. - Young lady, this may be the high point of Bart's life.
jokes.setup: Lisa's moral outrage at the contest
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual acceptance that this degradation is Bart's peak achievement
jokes.explanation: Dark family comedy with perfect character voices - Homer's low expectations for Bart

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is degrading! How could you let Bart enter such a contest?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, this is the best thing Bart's ever gonna do.

jokes.text: Homer and his amazing Redenbacher Dreamcoat with 'Kernel Knowledge'
jokes.punchline: Popcorn puns on 'Joseph's Dreamcoat' and 'carnal knowledge'
jokes.explanation: Layered puns work reasonably well but not exceptionally clever

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: We have a winner! Homer Simpson will perform his act: 'Joseph's Dream Coat' made entirely of popcorn!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's right! It's a coat of many colors... and many kernels!

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: And what's your act called?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: 'Carnal Knowledge'... because it's corn-al knowledge! Get it?

jokes.text: Oh, this is so embarrassing!
jokes.setup: Homer's popcorn act presumably goes wrong
jokes.punchline: Lisa's mortification at family public humiliation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa reaction but the embarrassment itself isn't shown so impact is limited

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey everybody, watch me make popcorn out of my belly button!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *starts jumping and wiggling around*

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I can't believe this is happening.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're doing it again!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We're never going back to this school.

jokes.text: Lisa, did anyone force you to come here today? - You.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's blunt correction of his false premise
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character dynamic - Lisa cutting through Homer's deflection

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why must I choose? Why can't I have both?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Because you can't, Dad. Life is full of choices, and you have to accept the consequences.

jokes.text: But it's me, Moe, wearing a sailor suit. Moe with a lolly. It's so out of character.
jokes.punchline: Meta-awareness that costume doesn't match his gruff personality
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor with Moe understanding character comedy theory while failing at it

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: What's the matter? Don't you like my costume?

dialogue.speaker: Audience
dialogue.line: Boo!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Oh, I see. A big tough bartender like me in a cute little fairy outfit... I guess that is pretty funny.

jokes.text: The winner is... me for being seen with you freaks
jokes.punchline: Judge declares himself winner while insulting all contestants
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing contempt from authority figure for participants

dialogue.speaker: Contest Judge
dialogue.line: And the winner of this year's contest is... me! You're all losers, every single one of you!

jokes.text: Well, you- A urinal cake! You, sir, have crossed the line.
jokes.punchline: The line-crossing item is a urinal cake
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation showing arbitrary moral boundaries in degraded situation

dialogue.speaker: Contest Judge
dialogue.line: Now this is outrageous! You can't possibly serve that!

dialogue.speaker: Contestant
dialogue.line: But it's just a urinal cake!

dialogue.speaker: Contest Judge
dialogue.line: A urinal cake?! Well, that changes everything. Delicious!

jokes.text: People, stop, stop! We're not animals!
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by literal ape calls from crowd
jokes.explanation: Good ironic timing with Lisa's civilization plea immediately contradicted

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Can't we all just try to be civilized and respectful to one another?

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Ooh ooh ooh! Ah ah ah! Eee eee eee!

jokes.text: Today, our town lost what remained of its fragile civility... drowned in a sea of low fat pudding
jokes.setup: Lisa's serious letter about town's degradation
jokes.punchline: Pompous language contrasted with ridiculous pudding riot cause
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between elevated language and absurd cause, fits Lisa's pretentious writing style

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I have written a letter to the Springfield Gazette about the town's moral decay and social degradation.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's nice, sweetie.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It begins: 'Dear Editor, I am compelled to address the alarming decline of civic responsibility in our fair municipality...'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Blah blah blah. Hey, did you hear? There was a riot at the Kwik-E-Mart.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: A riot? Over what? Economic injustice? Political oppression?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Pudding cups were on sale.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: ...I'm going to need a new letter.

jokes.text: Look, I got runner-up prize. - You won second place? - No, but I got it.
jokes.punchline: Casual admission he stole the runner-up prize despite not winning
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart moment - casual admission of theft with no shame

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Check it out, man. I won this sweet prize at the contest!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's great, Bart. What did you win it for?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, technically I came in second... but the first place prize was lame, so I just took this one instead.

jokes.text: Free boudoir photography. - Sweet!
jokes.punchline: Homer's enthusiasm for inappropriate photography prize
jokes.explanation: Sets up Homer's photo shoot subplot with characteristic poor judgment

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, look at this prize we won!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A photography contest? Let me see that. Ooh, inappropriate photographs of celebrities... Woo-hoo! Finally, a contest I can win!

jokes.text: We are a town of lowbrows, no brows and ignorami
jokes.punchline: Escalating classification of stupidity with 'ignorami' pseudo-Latin plural
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating wordplay with perfect Lisa pretentiousness and clever fake Latin

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, your ignorance isn't just limited to one subject—it's comprehensive. It spans multiple disciplines. In fact, I'd say you're not just ignorant, you're ignorami. And if there were more of you, you'd be ignoramuses. Or should that be ignorami? The Latin plural of ignoramus would technically be ignorami, making you part of a whole genus of ignoramus—ignorami collectively.

jokes.text: We have eight malls, but no symphony. Thirty-two bars, but no alternative theater. Thirteen stores that begin with 'Le Sex.'
jokes.punchline: Escalating specificity ending with absurdly precise sex shop count
jokes.explanation: Good escalating structure with specific absurd numbers, 'Le Sex' is particularly funny

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Springfield's cultural infrastructure is woefully inadequate. We have zero art museums, no symphony orchestra, and precisely forty-seven adult entertainment establishments.

jokes.text: Well, most of us
jokes.setup: Pig squealing and 'Yeehaw!' interrupting her letter
jokes.punchline: Lisa's resigned qualification of her improvement hopes
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and economy - Lisa's idealism immediately tempered by reality

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dear Mr. President, I hope this letter finds you well. I'm writing to express my concerns about—

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Yeehaw!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: ...as long as Ralph doesn't interrupt me.

jokes.text: Yes, there's a real think piece here about a bra sale
jokes.setup: Homer claiming to read something provocative
jokes.punchline: The 'think piece' is just a bra advertisement
jokes.explanation: Good Homer characterization - misunderstanding intellectual engagement

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, this is quite a think piece!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh... it's just a bra advertisement.

jokes.text: Everything but the opinion page. I don't need to be told what to think... by anyone living
jokes.punchline: Implication he only takes advice from dead people/religious figures
jokes.explanation: Excellent Flanders characterization revealing his religious fundamentalism through reading habits

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I only take my life advice from dead people and religious figures.

jokes.text: Yeah, I need to housebreak our new police dog. Plus, it couldn't hurt Ralphie to brush up on the fundamentals
jokes.punchline: Using newspaper both for dog toilet training AND his son's education
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Ralph's education being equivalent to dog training, perfect Wiggum obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, I've been using the newspaper two ways - to train the dog where to go to the bathroom, and to teach Ralph where NOT to go to the bathroom.

jokes.text: Daddy says I'm this close to living in the yard
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of potential banishment to yard for poor performance
jokes.explanation: Dark child abuse humor delivered with Ralph's characteristic innocent delivery

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm in danger.

jokes.text: Can you recommend any books for my mobile? - anything by Jane Austen - Thanks Lisa. I'll get right on it.
jokes.punchline: He wants books for a baby mobile, not reading
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Wiggum's misunderstanding and Lisa's oblivious helpful response

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Say, Lisa, you're a smart kid. Got any book recommendations?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, Chief Wiggum, I'd suggest some classic literature or educational non-fiction.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Great! I need something with big letters and lots of pictures.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Those are board books for toddlers, Chief Wiggum.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Exactly! I'm making a mobile for the baby. Gotta keep her entertained while I'm at the station.

jokes.text: Tell no one. And bring a dessert
jokes.punchline: Mundane potluck requirement breaks spy thriller tone
jokes.explanation: Perfect tonal whiplash from mysterious to domestic with dessert requirement

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An anonymous note? A secret rendezvous? This is it! I'm finally being recruited by the CIA!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, it says 'potluck dinner.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A code! A beautiful code!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It also says you need to bring a casserole.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My cover... blown.

jokes.text: Is that a pie or a quiche? - A pie. - You may enter.
jokes.punchline: Admission based solely on dessert type classification
jokes.explanation: Absurd intellectual gatekeeping reduced to pie identification

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Welcome to our secret organization. We only admit those with the highest intelligence.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What are the entrance requirements?

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: You must answer this: Are you a cake person or a pie person?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That determines membership?

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Absolutely. It's the only thing that really matters.

jokes.text: I know that. It's also a constellation visible only from the southern hemisphere
jokes.setup: Lisa showing she knows what Mensa is
jokes.punchline: Immediately one-upping with additional obscure knowledge
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa show-off moment with gratuitous additional knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mensa is an organization for people with IQ in the top 2%.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Of course, I'm also a member of the International High IQ Society, which is even more exclusive.

jokes.text: She's good. She's very good.
jokes.punchline: Impressed reaction as if she passed a test
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction but not particularly clever on its own

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Wow, you correctly identified all the constellations. You're clearly qualified to join Mensa.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Really? That's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: No, I'm just kidding. You failed.

jokes.text: Each the tops in his or her field... uh, except for Mel Lazarus
jokes.punchline: Casual dismissal of cartoonist as not actually successful
jokes.explanation: Mild humor at cartoonist's expense, somewhat mean-spirited

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Well, we have Stephen Hawking, Carl Sagan, Marilyn vos Savant...

jokes.text: Well, it was your delightfully condescending letter that put you over the top
jokes.punchline: Praising condescension as a positive trait
jokes.explanation: Good satire of intellectual elitism valuing condescension

dialogue.speaker: Lindsay Naegle
dialogue.line: We need someone with your unique ability to talk down to people without them realizing it. It's a gift, really. Most people get defensive, but you... you make condescension sound like concern. That's the kind of talent we're looking for.

jokes.text: Synergy, and books on how to cheat at bridge
jokes.punchline: Meaningless business buzzword plus petty cheating manuals
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of corporate BS and intellectual dishonesty

dialogue.speaker: Lindsay Naegle
dialogue.line: I produce meaningless business buzzwords and petty cheating manuals.

jokes.text: Rise to vote, sir
jokes.punchline: Political palindrome that works forwards and backwards
jokes.explanation: Clever palindrome with political meaning, well-crafted wordplay

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama!

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Now here's a political one: Was it a car or a cat I saw?

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: No wait... A Santa at NASA.

jokes.text: But 'rise to vote, sir' is a palindrome. - Good glavin. She's right.
jokes.punchline: Frink's surprised realization he was wrong
jokes.explanation: Lisa out-smarting the supposed experts, good character moment

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, Professor, 'racecar' is a palindrome, but 'Able was I ere I saw Elba' isn't because of the spacing and punctuation.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Gadzooks! You mean to tell me that in all my years of scientific inquiry, I've been classifying palindromes incorrectly? Zounds!

jokes.text: Uh, Geraldo Simpson
jokes.setup: Homer giving fake name for photo session
jokes.punchline: Choosing 'Geraldo' as alias for pornographic photos
jokes.explanation: Decent character humor with Homer's poor choice of pseudonym

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll use the name... Geraldo.

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Sir, these are pornographic photos.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Well, I don't, but the block association seems to
jokes.punchline: Reference to neighborhood complaints about his public nudity
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character moment revealing past nude incidents

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't have body image issues. The only complaint I get is from the neighbors about my public nudity.

jokes.text: They've gotten rid of the English literature section and replaced it with a make-your-own-sundae bar
jokes.punchline: Literal replacement of books with food service
jokes.explanation: Good satire of cultural degradation with specific absurd replacement

dialogue.speaker: Librarian
dialogue.line: We've decided to replace our books with a food court.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But what about reading?

dialogue.speaker: Librarian
dialogue.line: Reading? That's so 20th century. Now people can get their knowledge from the menu board.

jokes.text: I heard they got rid of the reference desk to make room for an air hockey table
jokes.punchline: Even more inappropriate replacement of information with games
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation but follows expected pattern without surprise

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Games? What games?

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: The library replaced all the reference books with video games.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: That's the best thing I've ever heard!

jokes.text: Even the microfilm? - Even the microfiche.
jokes.punchline: Confirming destruction of even the most obscure research materials
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation showing Lisa's research concerns

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh no! They've destroyed the library! All those books, all that knowledge... gone!

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Yes, and they've even burned the most obscure research materials. I had a first edition of 'Advanced Theoretical Physics for the Incredibly Gifted' in there!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is a catastrophe!

jokes.text: Would a nerd wear such an irreverent sweatshirt?
jokes.punchline: Sweatshirt presumably says something incredibly nerdy
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup where the defense probably proves the accusation

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I'll have you know this sweatshirt says 'I'm with stupid' and the arrow points outward.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: That's still nerdy.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: No it isn't! It's a classical reference to the ancient Roman tradition of—oh, who am I kidding, it says 'Revenge of the Nerds II: Nerds in Paradise.'

jokes.text: Oh, only one person in a million would find that funny. - Yes, we call that the Dennis Miller ratio
jokes.setup: Lisa reading the presumably nerdy sweatshirt
jokes.punchline: Meta-commentary on overly intellectual comedy
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor about comedy accessibility with perfect cultural reference timing

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This sweatshirt says 'I'm with stupid' with an arrow pointing up at my brain.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, that's quite the meta-commentary on overly intellectual humor, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I suppose that's the joke.

jokes.text: You tell me everything you can before the commercial's over
jokes.punchline: TV watching takes priority over family conversation
jokes.explanation: Good Homer parenting failure with TV priority over daughter

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So Lisa, how was school today?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, Dad, we learned about the history of—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's nice. Shh! My show is on.

jokes.text: Bup, bup, bup, bup
jokes.setup: Lisa starting to share her experience
jokes.punchline: Homer shushing her when commercial ends
jokes.explanation: Predictable but effective Homer moment prioritizing TV over family

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I want to tell you about what happened at school today.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shhh!

jokes.text: Are you sure you're a fully accredited and bonded pornographer?
jokes.punchline: Treating pornography as legitimate business requiring credentials
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist Homer logic applying business standards to inappropriate situation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not comfortable with this photo shoot.

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, it's completely professional.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, do you have any credentials?

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Credentials? What kind of credentials?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I dunno, like a license or something. I'm not taking my clothes off for just anybody.

jokes.text: Try not to speak. It's making your body ripple
jokes.punchline: Implication that Homer's speaking causes visible fat movement
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe humor about Homer's physical appearance

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Try not to talk during the shoot.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Because every time you speak, your stomach jiggles.

jokes.text: Stop looking down my blouse, Copernicus
jokes.punchline: Anachronistic complaint using wrong historical figure
jokes.explanation: Good nerdy humor with historical inaccuracy in role-playing context

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I cast a spell on you!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's not historically accurate. Galileo didn't use magic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's a fantasy game, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and in reality, Galileo invented the telescope, not spells. Get your anachronisms right!

jokes.text: Forsooth. Mine eyes doth rove of their own accord
jokes.punchline: Elaborate period language excuse for perverted behavior
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment with Comic Book Guy's pretentious language

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Ah, verily, I do perchance to gaze upon thy visage with great scholarly interest in the anatomical proportions as documented in classical Renaissance texts.

jokes.text: What part of 'beat it' didn't you understand? - I guess it would be the 'it.' I'm not exactly sure to what that refers.
jokes.punchline: Frink literally analyzing the grammar of a threat
jokes.explanation: Excellent Frink moment showing intellectual overthinking in dangerous situation

dialogue.speaker: Tough Guy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna break your legs!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Well, technically, the subject-verb agreement is correct, but your use of the future tense suggests a hypothetical rather than an imminent threat, so I'd rate your menacing on a scale of 1 to 10, about a 6.5.

jokes.text: How many gazebos do you she-males need?
jokes.punchline: Completely missing who the complainants are
jokes.explanation: Typical Wiggum confusion but relies on problematic terminology

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Alright, alright, settle down. I'm here to investigate the complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: So you're telling me the Kwik-E-Mart was robbed?

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: No, Chief, we called about the noise from the bar next door.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Right, right. Well, I'm gonna need to search the premises for stolen merchandise.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: But Chief—

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: No need to thank me, I'm just doing my job. You folks at Moe's need to keep it down!

jokes.text: Why do we live in a town where the smartest have no power and the stupidest run everything?
jokes.punchline: Direct statement of the show's political satire theme
jokes.explanation: Sharp political observation that sets up the episode's main theme

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny how power always flows to those who already have it, creating an endless cycle of inequality that the system is designed to perpetuate.

jokes.text: Faster, you moron
jokes.punchline: Insulting his horse while escaping
jokes.explanation: Typical Quimby arrogance extended to his transportation

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: I'm leaving this town and never coming back!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Giddyup, you broken-down nag! Move it!

jokes.text: 'We the people,' 'cruel and unusual,' blah, blah, blah. 'Ritual circumcision,' yak, yak, yak.
jokes.punchline: Dismissing constitutional concepts and finding random inappropriate content
jokes.explanation: Good Wiggum moment showing his casual dismissal of important concepts

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Let's see here... 'The Chief of Police shall be... blah blah blah... constitutional rights... blah blah blah...' Hey! It says here I can have a pet ostrich!

jokes.text: Or Walden Two. - Yeah, a real Candy Land! - Of the mind. The mind.
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy's childish suggestion corrected to sound intellectual
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing intellectual pretension vs. childish impulses

dialogue.speaker: Mensa Member 1
dialogue.line: We could base our utopia on Plato's Republic.

dialogue.speaker: Mensa Member 2
dialogue.line: Or perhaps Thomas More's island of Utopia.

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: What about Metropolis? It has Superman!

dialogue.speaker: Mensa Member 1
dialogue.line: That's not a real place.

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Well, what about... the planet Krypton before it exploded? It had advanced technology and culture.

dialogue.speaker: Mensa Member 2
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, a fictional advanced civilization. Quite intellectual.

jokes.text: You have been chosen to join the Justice Squadron... 8:00 a.m. Monday at the Municipal Fortress of Vengeance
jokes.punchline: Superhero comic book language for courthouse
jokes.explanation: Clever solution to civic engagement using comic book appeal

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I move that we find the defendant... GUILTY! *pow* *bam* *kapow*

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Young lady, this is a court of law, not a comic book.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Your Honor, with great jury power comes great jury responsibility!

jokes.text: So now, we just have the red and yellow lights
jokes.setup: Traffic optimization based on driver behavior
jokes.punchline: Eliminating green lights entirely
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic taking traffic observation to illogical conclusion

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I've analyzed the traffic patterns, and I've determined that eliminating green lights entirely will optimize flow.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So we just drive through red lights?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Precisely. No more stopping, no more waiting. It's the perfect solution!

jokes.text: Man, I'm making record time, if only I had someplace to be
jokes.punchline: Barney's lack of destinations makes efficiency pointless
jokes.explanation: Good irony about efficiency improvements for someone with no purpose

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: An efficient traffic system? What's the point? I don't have anywhere to go anyway!

jokes.text: And instead of chasing a rabbit, the dogs chase a diploma
jokes.punchline: Dogs literally chasing educational achievement
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual absurdity combining intellectual pretension with animal entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Dogs literally chasing educational achievement

jokes.text: Springfield has moved up to number 299 on the list of America's 300 most livable cities. Take that, East St. Louis.
jokes.punchline: Barely avoiding last place and targeting already struggling city
jokes.explanation: Good satire of civic boosterism with minimal improvement celebrated as victory

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Springfield has improved its standing in the national rankings! We've barely avoided last place, and we're setting our sights on Shelbyville!

jokes.text: It's time to get Homer-erotic!
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'homoerotic' using his own name
jokes.explanation: Decent pun working with Homer's cluelessness about what he's saying

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Now Homer, I want you to really feel the camera. Let the passion flow through you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... passion.

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Yes! This is turning into something quite homoerotic.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Homoerotic? That's not right. It should be Homererotic!

jokes.text: Remember this moment, people- 80 past 2:00 on April 47th. It's the dawn of an enlightened Springfield.
jokes.punchline: Completely nonsensical time and date system
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor with Skinner's bureaucratic pride in meaningless change

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: As principal, I'm proud to announce Springfield Elementary will be the first school to adopt metric time. Ten hours per day, one hundred minutes per hour, one hundred seconds per minute.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, that's just crazy talk!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Not crazy, Flanders. Scientific. We'll also implement the metric calendar: ten months per year, ten days per month, ten weeks per month...

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Wait, so how many days are in a month?

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Why not both? Then everybody's happy. - Oh, yeah. Everyone's real happy then!
jokes.setup: Compromise suggestion for shadow puppet theater
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic response to impossible-to-please situation
jokes.explanation: Good character friction showing intellectual committee dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: Why don't we compromise? We'll do shadow puppets of things that are both light AND dark.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, that's impossible. You can't have something that's simultaneously illuminated and in shadow.

dialogue.speaker: Mensa member
dialogue.line: I know. That's why I suggested it — I figured you'd finally be happy with something that makes no sense.

jokes.text: Oh, a sarcasm detector. That's a real useful invention.
jokes.punchline: Self-defeating sarcastic comment about sarcasm detector
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-referential joke where sarcasm about sarcasm detector triggers the device

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Behold! My sarcasm detector! It can identify any sarcastic remark with 100% accuracy!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Oh yes, because that will definitely work.

jokes.text: It's like the set of some high-class porno film. - No, no. It's just our basement.
jokes.punchline: Homer correcting her assumption about location/purpose
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Marge's innocent misunderstanding of the setup

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh Homer, this is so elegant. Are we at the country club?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, we're at Krusty Burger. I just put a candle in your Krabby Patty.

jokes.text: But I was gonna score. - No, you weren't.
jokes.punchline: Marge's blunt assessment of his chances
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy with Marge's realistic assessment of Homer's romantic skills

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Every time I try to talk, someone interrupts me!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, no one's interrupting you. You just never have anything worth listening to.

jokes.text: Also, boxing, both kick and the kind with the gloves. And hockey, football, push-ups... and anything in general where you have to take off your shirt, which is embarrassing
jokes.punchline: Escalating from violence to personal body image issues
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Frink's personal insecurities driving policy

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Well, we've banned all the violent sports - boxing, football, hockey... And now we're banning any sport that could lead to body image issues.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: So that means no more gymnastics, figure skating, or synchronized swimming.

jokes.text: For many of you, this will mean much less breeding. For me, much, much more.
jokes.punchline: Frink admitting he never gets any action normally
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Frink's sexual inadequacy driving authoritarian policy

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Well, you see, if we restrict breeding, the population will decrease exponentially, which means fewer opportunities for, uh, mating and procreation.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Of course, in my case, that would be no change whatsoever! Goyoyoyoy!

jokes.text: You should all do what I say. My I.Q. is 199, for crying out glavin. 198. 197.
jokes.punchline: IQ dropping in real time as he speaks
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation showing intelligence decreasing as power corrupts

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: My IQ is so astronomically high that I can calculate the exact rate at which it's dropping while I speak to you people.

jokes.text: Big deal. My I.Q. is 280.
jokes.punchline: Stephen Hawking one-upping everyone with impossible IQ
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with celebrity cameo effectively ending the IQ contest

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: My IQ is 160.

dialogue.speaker: Mensa Member 1
dialogue.line: Mine is 187.

dialogue.speaker: Mensa Member 2
dialogue.line: Mine is 192.

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: Mine is 1,860.

dialogue.speaker: Mensa Member 1
dialogue.line: That's impossible!

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: So is talking with this voice box, yet here I am.

jokes.text: I wanted to see your utopia. But now I see it is more of a Fruitopia
jokes.punchline: Pun on failed drink brand suggesting their utopia is artificial/commercial
jokes.explanation: Clever cultural reference pun comparing failed society to failed beverage

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: Your society was on the verge of collapse. Now, you are a model of stability and peace. I can only conclude that you have accepted a false reality and the alcohol has cloud your judgement.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Mmm... Buzz Cola.

jokes.text: Larry Flynt is right! You guys stink!
jokes.punchline: Random invocation of pornography publisher as moral authority
jokes.explanation: Absurd cultural reference with Larry Flynt as unlikely voice of reason

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: We're gonna rise up against all these so-called intellectuals!

dialogue.speaker: Mob member
dialogue.line: Yeah! They think they're better than us!

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: But we got morality on our side. Larry Flynt said so!

jokes.text: That's too clever. You're one of them!
jokes.punchline: Anti-intellectual mob rejecting their own member for being clever
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on anti-intellectualism turning against itself

dialogue.speaker: Mob Member 1
dialogue.line: Hey, that's a good one!

dialogue.speaker: Mob Member 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, a real play on words!

dialogue.speaker: Mob Member 3
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... did you just make a pun?

dialogue.speaker: Mob Member 1
dialogue.line: Yeah, so?

dialogue.speaker: Mob Member 2
dialogue.line: That means you think you're better than us!

dialogue.speaker: Mob Members
dialogue.line: Get him! Traitor!

jokes.text: Time for this Hawk to fly. - Wrong button.
jokes.punchline: Technical malfunction undermining cool departure
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy undermining Hawking's attempted cool exit

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: In accordance with my predictions, I shall depart this planet momentarily.

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking's Computer
dialogue.line: Does not compute.

jokes.text: Don't feel bad, Lisa. Sometimes the smartest of us can be the most childish. - Even you? - No, not me. Never.
jokes.setup: Hawking offering wisdom about intellectual maturity
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting his own lesson with arrogance
jokes.explanation: Excellent irony with Hawking proving his own point while denying it

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: The mark of intellectual maturity is being able to entertain a thought without accepting it.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wow, that's a great point.

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: Of course it is, I'm Stephen Hawking.

jokes.text: Hey, you read that off my screen
jokes.punchline: Hawking accusing her of plagiarizing his computerized wisdom
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about computer-assisted wisdom and intellectual property

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I think that if we could just all try to understand each other a little bit better, the world would be a much nicer place.

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: Oh, come on. That's my line. I've been saying that for years through my computer.

jokes.text: Your theory of a doughnut-shaped universe is intriguing, Homer. I may have to steal it.
jokes.punchline: World's smartest man considering stealing Homer's drunk wisdom
jokes.explanation: Excellent reversal with Homer's simple thinking impressing genuine genius

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: Your theory of quantum mechanics combined with your understanding of donuts is fascinating. Perhaps I've been approaching physics all wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking
dialogue.line: In fact, I'm considering abandoning my career to follow your path of drunken wisdom.

jokes.text: Wow. I can't believe someone I never heard of... is hanging out with a guy like me.
jokes.punchline: Homer's complete ignorance of Hawking's fame
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment showing his obliviousness to cultural significance

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Stephen Hawking? Who's that? Is he one of the guys from that band?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, he's only the most brilliant physicist alive.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A physicist? Oh. Well, that explains why I don't know who he is.

jokes.text: I didn't say that. - Yes, I did. D'oh.
jokes.setup: Homer denying he said he'd pay the bar tab
jokes.punchline: Hawking's voice box contradicting him with Homer's own catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Brilliant ending with technology using Homer's voice against him

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I never said I'd pay the tab!

dialogue.speaker: Stephen Hawking's Voice Box
dialogue.line: D'oh!

tmdb_overview: Mayor Quimby flees Springfield, leaving the town chapter of Mensa in charge. As the group's new power goes to their heads, they take their utopian plans too far and the townspeople revolt.
insight: Mid-tier Simpsons: 83 jokes in 22 minutes, but weak craft and impact drag the score down.
